title: Apache Airflow: Beginner Track
description: Beginner-first Airflow curriculum covering DAGs, scheduling, sensors, configuration, monitoring, and a capstone orchestration project.

chapters.title: What Is Airflow? – The Big Picture
chapters.description: Understand what Airflow does, why it exists, how it fits into a data platform, and get a working local environment running.

blocks.content: Before Airflow, NordGrid’s data team ran everything manually. They had a collection of Python scripts, each doing one job: one script downloaded meter files, another cleaned them, another loaded them into the warehouse, another ran quality checks, and another sent a report to the billing team.
The team ran these scripts by hand every morning. Or they used cron jobs – the built-in scheduler on Linux systems that can run a command at a set time.

# NordGrid's old crontab (Linux scheduler)
# Run meter download at 6:00 AM
0 6 * * * python /scripts/download_meters.py
# Run meter cleaning at 6:30 AM (hope download finished by then!)
30 6 * * * python /scripts/clean_meters.py
# Run quality check at 7:00 AM
0 7 * * * python /scripts/validate_meters.py
# Run warehouse load at 7:30 AM
30 7 * * * python /scripts/load_meters.py
# Run billing report at 8:00 AM
0 8 * * * python /scripts/billing_report.py
This looks tidy, but it has five serious problems:

Problem 1: No dependency awareness. The cron jobs run at fixed times, not in response to the previous step finishing. If the meter download takes 45 minutes instead of the usual 20 minutes, the cleaning script starts at 6:30 AM on a file that has not finished downloading yet.
Problem 2: No retry logic. If the SFTP server hiccups at 6:01 AM and the download fails, the entire day’s pipeline is dead. Nobody retries automatically.
Problem 3: No visibility. There is no dashboard showing which scripts ran, which succeeded, which failed, and how long each took.
Problem 4: No history. There is no record of past runs. If the billing team says ‘Wednesday’s numbers look wrong,’ the data team cannot easily check what happened on Wednesday.
Problem 5: No scalability. NordGrid started with one data source and five scripts. Now they have five data sources and twenty scripts. The crontab has become a tangled mess.
Airflow solves all five problems. Let’s see how.
Apache Airflow is a tool that does three things:

items: It schedules tasks. You tell Airflow: ‘Run this pipeline every day at 6 AM.’ Airflow creates a run for each day, on schedule, automatically.

It manages dependencies. You tell Airflow: ‘Do not start cleaning until the download is finished.’ Airflow tracks which tasks have completed and only starts a task when all of its dependencies have succeeded.
It monitors everything. Every task execution is recorded: when it started, how long it took, whether it succeeded or failed, what it logged, and what values it returned.

blocks.content: The Traffic Controller Analogy

The best way to think about Airflow is as a traffic controller at an airport. The traffic controller does not fly the planes. The traffic controller does not load the cargo. What the traffic controller does is: decide the order, decide the timing, monitor the status, handle problems, and keep records.

Airflow is the traffic controller for your data platform. Your Python scripts, Spark jobs, SQL queries, and API calls are the planes. Airflow decides when they run, in what order, and what happens when something goes wrong. It does not do the data processing itself – it orchestrates the systems that do.
This is the most important concept in the entire course. We call it the conductor pattern: Airflow conducts the orchestra, but it does not play the instruments.
The Four Key Vocabulary Words

rows: A pipeline. A set of tasks with a defined order.
The daily meter readings pipeline: download → clean → validate → load.
A single unit of work inside a pipeline. One step.
Download the meter CSV from SFTP is one task.
A template that defines what type of work a task does.
PythonOperator means run a Python function.
One execution of a pipeline for a specific date.
The January 15th run of the meter pipeline.

blocks.content: Four words: DAG, task, operator, DAG Run. Every other concept in Airflow builds on these four.

sections.title: Where Airflow Fits in a Data Platform

blocks.content: A data platform has five layers. Understanding where Airflow sits helps you understand what it does and what it does not do.

rows: Where data originates. SFTP servers, REST APIs, databases.
No. Airflow does not generate data.
Decides when tasks run, in what order, with what dependencies.
Transforms, cleans, aggregates data. Spark, dbt, SQL.
No. Airflow triggers these but does not do the processing.
Stores data at rest. S3, Delta Lake, PostgreSQL.
No. Airflow does not store data.
Where humans and systems read processed data. Dashboards, notebooks.
No. Airflow does not visualize data.

blocks.content: Airflow occupies exactly one layer: orchestration. It sits between data sources and processing engines. It is a control plane, not a data plane. The control plane makes decisions (what runs when). The data plane does the work (moves and transforms bytes).

sections.title: The Airflow UI – A Guided Tour

blocks.content: When you open Airflow in your browser (typically at http://localhost:8080), you see the DAGs page. This is your home screen listing every pipeline.
The DAGs List Page

The DAGs list shows one row per pipeline. At a glance, you can see which pipelines exist, how often they run, when they last ran, and whether recent runs succeeded or failed. This is the visibility that cron jobs never gave NordGrid’s team.
The Graph View

Click on a DAG name and you see the Graph View – a visual diagram of the pipeline’s tasks and their dependencies. Each box is a task. The arrows show dependencies. Tasks are color-coded by status: green means success, red means failed, yellow means running.
The Grid View
The Grid View shows the same tasks but across time. Each column is a DAG Run. Each row is a task. The cells are colored by status. This lets you see patterns across many runs at a glance.
Task Logs

Every task writes a log. Click a task to read its log containing: the task start time, your print statements, error messages, and the task end time and duration. Learning to read task logs is a skill you will develop throughout this course.
You are going to install Airflow on your own machine using Docker. Docker gives you a complete Airflow environment – scheduler, webserver, database – with a single command.
Step-by-Step Installation

mkdir airflow-nordgrid
cd airflow-nordgrid
mkdir -p dags logs plugins
Download the official Docker Compose file, set the Airflow user ID, initialize the database, and start Airflow:

curl -LfO 'https://airflow.apache.org/docs/apache-airflow/stable/docker-compose.yaml'
echo -e "AIRFLOW_UID=$(id -u)" > .env
docker compose up airflow-init
docker compose up -d
Open http://localhost:8080. Log in with username airflow and password airflow. You should see the DAGs list page with example DAGs pre-loaded.
Useful Docker Commands

rows: Start all Airflow services in the background
Stop all services. Data is preserved.
Stop all services AND delete the database. Full reset.

sections.title: The Pieces of Airflow – Scheduler, Webserver, Database

blocks.content: Airflow has four main components. Understanding what each one does will help you reason about how your pipelines work.
The Scheduler – The Brain
The scheduler runs continuously and does three things in a loop: reads your DAG files (every 30 seconds), creates DAG Runs when it is time, and schedules tasks by checking dependencies.
The Webserver – The Dashboard
The webserver is what you see at http://localhost:8080. It reads the metadata database and presents it as a visual interface. It does not run any tasks or make scheduling decisions.
The Metadata Database – The Memory
Airflow stores all state in PostgreSQL: every DAG definition, every DAG Run, every task instance, connections, and variables. The database is the single source of truth.
The Executor – The Muscle
The executor actually runs your tasks. When the scheduler decides a task is ready, it hands it to the executor, which finds a worker to run it.

sections.title: Your dags/ Folder – Where Pipelines Live

blocks.content: Every pipeline starts as a Python file in the dags/ folder. The scheduler scans this folder periodically, reads every .py file, and looks for DAG objects.
Creating Your First DAG File

# dags/hello_nordgrid.py
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from datetime import datetime

def say_hello():
    print('Hello from NordGrid Energy!')
    print('The grid is running. All systems nominal.')

with DAG(
    dag_id='hello_nordgrid',
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    schedule=None,
    catchup=False,
    tags=['tutorial'],
) as dag:
    greet = PythonOperator(
        task_id='say_hello',
        python_callable=say_hello,
    )
A Critical Concept: Declaration, Not Script

Your DAG file is not a script that runs top to bottom. The scheduler reads it every 30 seconds to understand what DAGs and tasks exist. The actual task execution happens separately. Keep the top level of your DAG file light – heavy imports belong inside task functions.
Every module builds on the same fictional company. NordGrid Energy operates smart grid infrastructure across four regions: NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, and WEST.
The Data Sources
The Data Layers

rows: Raw data, exactly as arrived. No transformations.
Cleaned, deduplicated, validated data. Types correct.
Business-ready aggregated tables. Star schema for analysts.

blocks.content: This is the platform you will build across 10 modules. The patterns you learn – scheduling, dependencies, sensors, retries, dynamic tasks – apply to any industry.
Airflow is the most widely adopted orchestration tool, but it helps to understand how it compares to alternatives.
Airflow vs. Cron Jobs
Airflow vs. Fabric Pipelines
Airflow remains the industry standard due to ecosystem size, job market demand, managed service availability (MWAA, Cloud Composer, Astronomer), and community size.
Time to put everything together. Trigger the hello_nordgrid DAG, watch it execute, and read the task logs.
Steps

items: Unpause the DAG by clicking the toggle in the UI.
Click the play button to trigger a manual run.
Watch the task turn from grey to green in the Graph View.
Click the task and read the Logs to see your print output.
Go to the Grid View and verify one column of green cells.

blocks.content: Mini-Exercise: Modify and Re-run

items: Change the print messages in hello_nordgrid.py.
Save the file. Wait 30–60 seconds for the scheduler to re-parse.
Trigger the DAG again from the UI.
Read the logs for the new run. Verify your new messages appear.
Go to the Grid View. You should now see two runs, both green.

blocks.content: You now understand:

items: The problem Airflow solves – it replaces manual scripts and cron jobs with dependency management, retries, visibility, history, and alerting.
What Airflow is – a scheduler, dependency manager, and monitoring system. The traffic controller for your data platform.
The four key terms – DAG (pipeline), task (step), operator (template), DAG Run (one execution).
Where Airflow fits – the orchestration layer between sources and processing engines. Control plane, not data plane.
The Airflow UI – DAGs list, Graph View, Grid View, and task logs.
How to install Airflow – Docker Compose gives you a complete environment.
The four components – scheduler (brain), webserver (dashboard), metadata database (memory), executor (muscle).
DAG files are declarations, not scripts – heavy code belongs inside task functions.
The NordGrid scenario – the platform you will build across 10 modules.
How Airflow compares – more powerful than cron, more portable than Fabric Pipelines.

blocks.content: In Module 2, you write your first real DAG from scratch – a pipeline with multiple tasks, dependencies between them, and code that processes NordGrid energy data.

chapters.title: Your First DAG – Hello Energy Grid
chapters.description: Write your first DAG from scratch with multiple tasks, dependencies, default_args, and the complete NordGrid meter readings pipeline.

sections.title: What Is a DAG? – Recipes and Dependencies

blocks.content: In Module 1 we defined a DAG as ‘a pipeline.’ That is good enough to get started, but now let’s understand what the letters actually mean, because the concept behind them is the reason Airflow works the way it does.
DAG stands for Directed Acyclic Graph. Let’s break that down:
Graph – A collection of things (called nodes) connected by lines (called edges). In Airflow, tasks are nodes and dependencies are edges.
Directed – The connections have a direction. Task A points to Task B, meaning ‘A must finish before B starts.’ It is a one-way arrow.

Acyclic – There are no loops. You cannot follow the arrows and end up back where you started. If Task A depends on B and B depends on A, neither can ever start – it is a deadlock. Airflow does not allow this.
The Recipe Analogy
A DAG is like a recipe for cooking a meal. Consider making a NordGrid engineer’s favourite: a hearty Norwegian fish stew.

items: Step 1: Chop the vegetables. (No dependencies – you can start immediately.)
Step 2: Fillet the fish. (No dependencies – can run in parallel with Step 1.)
Step 3: Make the broth. (Depends on Step 1 – needs the chopped vegetables.)
Step 4: Add fish to broth. (Depends on Steps 2 AND 3.)
Step 5: Serve. (Depends on Step 4.)

blocks.content: Now map this to NordGrid’s daily meter pipeline: [Download meters] → [Clean data] → [Validate quality] → [Load to warehouse]. Four tasks. Each depends on the previous one. This is a simple linear DAG.
Why Acyclic Matters

Imagine if the ‘validate quality’ task could send data back to the ‘clean data’ task for re-cleaning. That creates a loop: clean → validate → clean → validate… forever. The pipeline never finishes. Airflow prevents this by checking for cycles at parse time.

sections.title: Anatomy of a DAG File – Line by Line

blocks.content: Let’s write a real DAG for NordGrid’s daily meter readings pipeline. This DAG has four tasks: start the pipeline, extract meter readings, validate the data, and mark the pipeline as complete.

# dags/energy_meter_daily.py
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from airflow.operators.empty import EmptyOperator
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

default_args = {
    'owner': 'grid-engineering',
    'depends_on_past': False,
    'retries': 2,
    'retry_delay': timedelta(minutes=5),
    'email_on_failure': False,
}

def extract_meter_readings():
    print('Connecting to NordGrid SFTP server...')
    print('Downloaded 4,200,000 rows from meters_north_20260315.csv')

def validate_meter_data():
    total_rows = 4_200_000
    null_rows = 1_234
    valid_rows = total_rows - null_rows
    print(f'Quality score: {valid_rows / total_rows * 100:.2f}%')

with DAG(
    dag_id='energy_meter_daily',
    default_args=default_args,
    description='Daily pipeline: extract and validate NordGrid meter readings',
    schedule=None,
    start_date=datetime(2026, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    tags=['energy', 'meters', 'nordgrid'],
) as dag:
    start = EmptyOperator(task_id='start')
    extract = PythonOperator(task_id='extract_meter_readings', python_callable=extract_meter_readings)
    validate = PythonOperator(task_id='validate_meter_data', python_callable=validate_meter_data)
    end = EmptyOperator(task_id='end')
    start >> extract >> validate >> end
That is 60 lines of Python. It defines a complete pipeline with four tasks and clear dependencies. Let’s examine every section.
Section 1: Imports

Four imports. DAG represents your pipeline. PythonOperator runs a Python function. EmptyOperator does nothing (structural marker). datetime and timedelta handle dates and durations. These imports are all fast and lightweight – the scheduler re-reads your file every 30 seconds.
Section 2: Task Functions

These are regular Python functions. There is nothing special about them. In a real pipeline, extract_meter_readings would connect to an SFTP server and download a file. validate_meter_data would read the file and count invalid rows.
Key insight: Your task functions are where your business logic lives. The DAG definition is the orchestration wrapper. Keeping them separate makes your code easier to test, read, and change.
The default_args dictionary defines rules that apply to every task in the DAG, unless a specific task overrides them. Think of default_args as the company policy at NordGrid.

default_args = {
    'owner': 'grid-engineering',
    'depends_on_past': False,
    'retries': 2,
    'retry_delay': timedelta(minutes=5),
    'email_on_failure': False,
}
owner – A label that identifies who is responsible for this DAG. This appears in the UI and is useful for filtering.
depends_on_past – When False (the most common setting), each day’s run is independent. Monday’s extract can run even if Sunday’s extract failed.
retries – A value of 2 means: try once, and if it fails, try again up to 2 more times (3 attempts total). NordGrid’s SFTP server occasionally hiccups during heavy load.
retry_delay – timedelta(minutes=5) means wait 5 minutes after a failure before trying again. This gives transient issues time to resolve.
email_on_failure – Whether Airflow sends an email when a task fails. Set to False for now because we have not configured an email server.
The Full List of default_args Options

rows: Who is responsible for this DAG
Must yesterday succeed before today runs?
How many times to retry on failure
Send email when a task fails
Python function to call on failure
Maximum time a task can run

blocks.content: The PythonOperator is the workhorse of Airflow. It takes a Python function and runs it as a task.

extract = PythonOperator(
    task_id='extract_meter_readings',
    python_callable=extract_meter_readings,
)
Three things happen: a task is created, it gets an ID (task_id) that appears in the UI, and it is linked to a function (python_callable).
A Common Mistake: Calling the Function at Definition Time

# WRONG - Do NOT do this
python_callable=extract_meter_readings()  # <-- parentheses!

# CORRECT - No parentheses
python_callable=extract_meter_readings
Adding parentheses calls the function immediately when the scheduler parses the file. The function would run every 30 seconds, not once per day.
Passing Arguments to Your Function

# op_kwargs is preferred because arguments are named
extract = PythonOperator(
    task_id='extract_north',
    python_callable=extract_for_region,
    op_kwargs={'region': 'NORTH', 'load_date': '2026-03-15'},
)
Dependencies define the order in which tasks run. The >> operator (think of it as ‘then’) declares dependencies.
start >> extract >> validate >> end
Read this as: start runs first, then extract runs, then validate runs, then end runs. Each task waits for the previous one to finish successfully.
Multiple Dependency Patterns
Linear chain: a >> b >> c >> d
Fan-out (one task triggers many parallel tasks):
extract >> [clean_north, clean_south, clean_east, clean_west]
Fan-in (multiple tasks must finish before one can start):
[clean_north, clean_south, clean_east, clean_west] >> quality_gate
Diamond (fan-out then fan-in): the most common pattern in production pipelines. We will build increasingly complex diamonds throughout the course.
The EmptyOperator creates a task that does absolutely nothing. It succeeds immediately and moves on.
Why use it? Visual structure (clear start/end markers), convergence points in diamond patterns, and future expansion placeholders.
EmptyOperator vs DummyOperator: In older tutorials you will see DummyOperator. It was renamed to EmptyOperator in Airflow 2.4. Both still work, but use EmptyOperator in new code.

sections.title: Running Your DAG – Trigger, Monitor, Celebrate

blocks.content: You have written the energy_meter_daily DAG. Now let’s run it.
Three Ways to Trigger
Method 1: The UI play button. Method 2: The CLI (docker compose exec airflow-worker airflow dags trigger energy_meter_daily). Method 3: The REST API.
Watch the Colors Change

rows: Task exists but has not been evaluated yet
Dependencies are met. Waiting for a worker slot
Sent to the executor. A worker will pick it up soon
A worker is executing the task right now
Task raised an exception or timed out
Task failed but has retries remaining

blocks.content: In a healthy run, you will see each task turn from grey to teal to lime to dark green, one after another, left to right.
Task logs are your window into what actually happened during execution. At NordGrid, when the billing team calls at 8:15 AM saying ‘where’s today’s data?’, your first action is always: open the logs.
Anatomy of a Task Log

The timestamp tells you exactly when each log line was produced. The source ({taskinstance.py}) tells you which Airflow module generated the line. ‘Starting attempt 1 of 3’ tells you the current attempt number. Your print output is where you verify your code ran correctly. ‘Marking task as SUCCESS’ confirms the task completed without errors.
What a Failed Task Log Looks Like
When a task fails, the log shows the full Python traceback, the error message, and ‘Marking task as UP_FOR_RETRY. Next retry in 300 seconds.’

blocks.title: Debugging tip
blocks.content: Always start at the bottom of the log and scroll up. The most useful information – the error message and the final state – is at the end.
Following these practices from the start will save you from painful debugging sessions later.
Naming Conventions

rows: Tells you the domain, source, and frequency

blocks.content: The Top-Level Code Rule

# GOOD: Heavy import inside the task function
def process_meter_data():
    import pandas as pd  # Only runs when the task executes
    df = pd.read_csv('/data/meters.csv')

# BAD: Heavy import at the top level
import pandas as pd  # Runs every 30 seconds by the scheduler!
Add Documentation

with DAG(
    dag_id='energy_meter_daily',
    description='Daily pipeline: extract and validate NordGrid meter readings',
    doc_md="""
    ## NordGrid Meter Readings Pipeline
    **Owner:** Grid Engineering Team
    **Schedule:** Daily at 06:00 UTC
    **SLA:** Must complete by 08:00 UTC for billing
    """,
    ...
) as dag:
The doc_md content renders as Markdown in the Airflow UI when someone clicks the DAG’s ‘Doc’ tab.
Time to build something on your own. Create a DAG called energy_meter_checkpoint with six tasks:
[start] --> [download_file] --> [count_rows] --> [check_quality] --> [log_result] --> [end]
Requirements

items: DAG settings: dag_id 'energy_meter_checkpoint', schedule None, start_date Jan 1 2026, catchup False, tags ['energy', 'checkpoint', 'module2']
default_args: owner 'grid-engineering', retries 1, retry_delay 3 minutes
download_file: Print download messages with file name and size
count_rows: Define total_rows = 4_200_000, print with comma formatting
check_quality: Calculate valid percentage, print PASS or FAIL based on 99% threshold
log_result: Print completion message with current timestamp
start and end use EmptyOperator

blocks.content: Verification Steps

items: Save the file, wait for it to appear in the UI
Unpause and trigger a manual run
Open the Graph View, verify six tasks in a linear chain
Watch all six tasks turn green
Click check_quality and verify quality percentage in logs
Go to the Grid View, verify one column of green cells

blocks.content: Bonus Challenges
Bonus 1: Make check_quality intentionally fail when quality is below 99.99%. Watch the retry mechanism. Bonus 2: Add a tag and filter the DAGs list. Bonus 3: Add doc_md to the DAG.
You now understand:

items: What a DAG actually is – a Directed Acyclic Graph: tasks with directional dependencies and no cycles
The anatomy of a DAG file – imports, default_args, task functions, the with DAG() context manager, task definitions, dependency chain
default_args – company policy for your pipeline: owner, retries, retry_delay, depends_on_past
PythonOperator – pass the function without parentheses. Use op_kwargs for named arguments
The >> operator – a >> b means ‘a must finish before b starts.’ Supports linear, fan-out, fan-in, and diamonds
EmptyOperator – visual anchor and convergence point
Three ways to trigger a DAG – UI play button, CLI, REST API
How to read task logs – timestamps, attempt numbers, print output, SUCCESS/FAILED marker
DAG file best practices – descriptive names, one DAG per file, light top-level code, doc_md

blocks.content: In Module 3, we go deeper into operators: BashOperator, SQLExecuteQueryOperator, the TaskFlow API, the context dictionary, XCom, and the conductor pattern.

chapters.title: Tasks and Operators – The Workers on the Grid
chapters.description: Master PythonOperator, BashOperator, SQLExecuteQueryOperator, the context dictionary, XCom data passing, and the conductor pattern.

blocks.content: An operator is a class that defines a type of task. When you write PythonOperator(task_id='extract', python_callable=my_func), you are creating a task instance from the PythonOperator template.

The analogy: an operator is a job description. A task is a person hired for that job. The job description says ‘this role runs Python functions.’ The task says ‘specifically, run the extract_meter_readings function.’
Common Operators

rows: Call an API, validate data, compute statistics
Runs a shell command or script
Download a file via curl, run an ETL script
Runs a SQL query against any database
INSERT readings, run COUNT for quality checks
Submits a job to a Spark cluster
Returns which task to execute next
Check if weekday; if yes, run pricing; if no, skip
After Silver finishes, trigger Gold layer

blocks.content: This module focuses on the first four: PythonOperator, BashOperator, EmptyOperator, and SQLExecuteQueryOperator.
The PythonOperator is the operator you will use most often because Python can do almost anything: call APIs, parse files, run computations, send alerts.
Example 1: Counting Rows

def count_meter_rows():
    import csv
    file_path = '/data/landing/meters/2026-03-15/meters_north.csv'
    with open(file_path, 'r') as f:
        reader = csv.reader(f)
        next(reader)  # Skip header
        row_count = sum(1 for _ in reader)
    print(f'Row count: {row_count:,}')
    return row_count  # Stored in XCom
Example 2: Checking All Regions Reported
When a task function raises an exception, Airflow marks the task as failed. If retries are configured, Airflow waits and tries again. This is how you build quality gates into your pipeline.
Example 3: Sending a Summary

def send_daily_summary(**context):
    dag_id = context['dag'].dag_id
    run_date = context['data_interval_start'].strftime('%Y-%m-%d')
    print(f'NordGrid Daily Summary - Date: {run_date}, DAG: {dag_id}')

sections.title: The Context Dictionary – What Airflow Tells Your Function

blocks.content: When Airflow calls your task function, it can pass a dictionary of information called the context. To receive it, add **context to your function signature.
Most Useful Context Keys

rows: Start of the data period this run covers
Building date-based file paths and SQL queries
Defining the upper bound of a date range
Quick date string for file paths
File names with compact date formats
Accessing the DAG ID or metadata
Pulling XCom values from upstream tasks

blocks.content: Using the Context in Practice
This function is date-aware. When Airflow runs it for March 15th, context['ds'] is '2026-03-15'. When it runs for March 16th, context['ds'] is '2026-03-16'. The function never uses datetime.now().
The BashOperator runs a shell command or script on the Airflow worker. Useful for downloading files with curl, running existing ETL scripts, or executing system commands.
Example: Downloading a File

from airflow.operators.bash import BashOperator

download_weather = BashOperator(
    task_id='download_weather_forecast',
    bash_command=(
        'curl -s -o /data/landing/weather/{{ ds }}/forecast.json '
        'https://api.weather.nordgrid.com/forecast?date={{ ds }}'
    ),
)
Notice: {{ ds }} is a Jinja template variable. Airflow replaces it with the actual date before executing. If the command returns exit code 0, the task succeeds. Any non-zero exit code means failure.
Example: Running a Python Script

run_etl_script = BashOperator(
    task_id='run_meter_etl',
    bash_command='python /scripts/etl/clean_meters.py --date {{ ds }} --region NORTH',
)
When to Use BashOperator vs PythonOperator

rows: Running an existing shell script or CLI tool
Using system commands (curl, rsync, aws cli)
The task is a one-line command
Needing to return structured data via XCom
Connecting to databases using Python libraries

blocks.content: Rule of thumb: If you are writing new code, use PythonOperator. If you are calling an existing script, use BashOperator. PythonOperator handles about 80% of tasks.
The SQLExecuteQueryOperator runs SQL against any database Airflow can connect to. It uses Connections to manage credentials securely.
Example: Inserting an Audit Log Entry

from airflow.providers.common.sql.operators.sql import SQLExecuteQueryOperator

log_start = SQLExecuteQueryOperator(
    task_id='log_pipeline_start',
    conn_id='postgres_default',
    sql="""
        INSERT INTO pipeline_audit_log (dag_id, run_date, event, created_at)
        VALUES ('energy_meter_daily', '{{ ds }}', 'PIPELINE_STARTED', NOW());
    """,
)
conn_id='postgres_default' references a Connection configured in the Airflow UI. The sql parameter supports Jinja templates. You can write any valid SQL: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE TABLE, SELECT.
Example: Running a SQL File

refresh_silver = SQLExecuteQueryOperator(
    task_id='refresh_silver_meters',
    conn_id='postgres_warehouse',
    sql='sql/silver_meters_refresh.sql',  # Path relative to dags/ folder
)

sections.title: The Conductor Pattern – Orchestrate, Don’t Process

blocks.content: This is the most important lesson in this module. Airflow should tell other systems what to do. It should not do the heavy work itself.
The Wrong Way: Processing Inside Airflow

# BAD PATTERN: Heavy data processing inside the Airflow task
def transform_bad(**context):
    import pandas as pd
    df = pd.read_csv('/data/meters.csv')  # 50 million rows in memory!
    df = df.dropna(subset=['daily_kwh'])
    df = df[df['daily_kwh'] >= 0]
    df.to_parquet('/data/silver/meters.parquet')
This will crash the Airflow worker (50M rows exceed worker memory), block other tasks, and not scale.
The Right Way: Triggering an External Engine
The actual data processing happens on the Spark cluster with 100 GB of distributed memory. The Airflow worker just sends the request and waits – using a few megabytes, not gigabytes.

blocks.title: The rule
blocks.content: Airflow orchestrates. External engines process. The conductor waves the baton. The orchestra plays the music.

sections.title: Returning Values from Tasks – XCom Basics

blocks.content: Tasks share small pieces of information through XCom (cross-communication). The simplest way: return a value from your task function.
Pushing Values
Pulling Values

def validate_meter_data(**context):
    ti = context['ti']
    extract_result = ti.xcom_pull(task_ids='extract_meter_readings')
    file_path = extract_result['file_path']
    row_count = extract_result['row_count']
    print(f'Validating {file_path} with {row_count:,} rows')
What NOT to Put in XCom

headers: Do NOT put in XCom (actual data)
rows: File contents: the entire CSV as a string

blocks.title: The rule
blocks.content: Pass references, not data. Tell the next task where to find the data, not the data itself.

sections.title: The TaskFlow API – Decorators Instead of Operators

blocks.content: The TaskFlow API offers a cleaner alternative using Python decorators. Data passing is automatic through function arguments.
Classic Style vs TaskFlow Style
Key differences: @dag replaces with DAG(). @task replaces PythonOperator. Data passing is automatic through function arguments. Dependencies are set by function calls, not >> operators.
You can mix both styles in the same DAG. Use @task for Python logic and classic operators for Bash, SQL, and external triggers.

sections.title: Choosing the Right Operator – A Decision Guide

blocks.content: When you need a new task, ask: what does it need to do, should work happen inside or outside Airflow, and does a specialized operator already exist?

If the task processes more than a few thousand rows or takes more than a few minutes of CPU time, it should happen outside Airflow (conductor pattern). Before writing custom code, check if Airflow has a built-in operator – there are over 2,000 in the provider packages.
Build a DAG called energy_multi_operator with three different operator types, XCom data passing, and the conductor pattern.
Structure: [start] --> [download_file] --> [count_and_validate] --> [log_to_db] --> [end]
Requirements

items: Task 1 (BashOperator): Simulate downloading a meter file using bash_command with {{ ds }} templates
Task 2 (PythonOperator): Read the CSV, count rows, validate quality, return results via XCom
Task 3 (SQLExecuteQueryOperator): Insert an audit record with conn_id='postgres_default'
start and end use EmptyOperator
Linear chain dependencies

blocks.content: Verification

items: Save, unpause, trigger. Verify four tasks plus start/end in Graph View.
BashOperator task shows 'File created' message in logs
PythonOperator task shows row counts and quality percentage
SQLExecuteQueryOperator may fail if audit table doesn't exist – that's expected and realistic
Click count_and_validate XCom tab, verify the return dictionary is visible

blocks.content: Bonus: Fix the SQL task by adding a CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS task. Rewrite using TaskFlow API. Make the quality check raise an exception below 50%.
You now understand:

items: What an operator is – a template for a type of work. PythonOperator runs Python, BashOperator runs shell commands, SQLExecuteQueryOperator runs SQL.
PythonOperator in depth – counting rows, checking regions, raising exceptions for quality gates, returning results.
The context dictionary – **context gives you ds (the date), data_interval_start, ti (for XCom), params, and more.
BashOperator – runs shell commands with {{ ds }} Jinja templates. Non-zero exit codes = failure.
SQLExecuteQueryOperator – runs SQL via conn_id. Supports templates and .sql files.
The conductor pattern – Airflow orchestrates, external engines process. Never load 50 million rows inside an Airflow task.
XCom basics – return values to push, ti.xcom_pull() to read. Pass references, not data.
The TaskFlow API – @dag and @task decorators. Automatic data passing. Mix with classic operators freely.
Choosing the right operator – PythonOperator for custom logic, BashOperator for shell, SQL for databases, check for specialized operators first.

Understanding platform health check is essential for building reliable data pipelines with Apache Airflow. This lesson provides hands-on examples using NordGrid’s meter readings, SCADA telemetry, and weather forecast data.
